prediction: Faster aerial arrival does not equal better situational comprehension. Response time measures detection (SA Level 1), not understanding (SA Level 2).
evidence: Elizabeth: 94s response (fastest) but 31% success rate (lowest). Speed-accuracy tradeoff confirms Level 1 does not improve Level 2.

prediction: 234 sec avg response is fast for aerial arrival but measures Level 1 only. No data on Level 2/3 outcomes.
evidence: Chula Vista: Fast response confirmed, but no comprehension outcome data available.

prediction: 94 sec avg response tests whether speed correlates with mission success.
evidence: Elizabeth: 94s response is 2.5x faster than Chula Vista but with lower success rate — confirms inverse speed-accuracy relationship.

prediction: Missions over 20 min should show degraded operator performance. Avg flight time 172 sec (Chula Vista) is below threshold.
evidence: Chula Vista avg flight 172s is below 20-min threshold. However Cincinnati shows 8% of flights exceed 20 min, confirming vigilance risk.

prediction: 10,000+ missions means operators have extensive experience — automaticity predicts faster but potentially biased threat evaluation.
evidence: Chula Vista: 10K+ missions over 4 years represents the highest operator exposure of any US DFR program.

prediction: 1,390 deployments in 7 months — moderate experience. Automaticity may not yet be fully developed.
evidence: Elizabeth: Moderate deployment volume supports automaticity still emerging.

prediction: Concentrated DFR deployments in specific neighborhoods predict lower perceived legitimacy and reduced community cooperation.
evidence: No community perception data available for any city. FOIA/survey data needed.

prediction: AI anomaly detection should show no time-dependent decrement (unlike human operators). If it does, reveals architectural bias.
evidence: Video benchmark analysis pending NIBRS data access.

prediction: PoseC3D classifies actions from skeleton data — tests whether movement patterns alone carry sufficient intent signal.
Self-supervised VideoMAE should discover event boundaries matching human-annotated transitions in surveillance footage.
Experienced operators maintain vigilance longer — DFR mission success rate should increase with operator tenure.
90% city coverage goal produces maximum surveillance visibility. Tests whether comprehensive coverage improves or degrades community trust.
evidence: Cincinnati: Spatial concentration index 0.452 — 45.2% of flights within 1 sigma of center.

prediction: 'Suspicious person' category creates vulnerability to over-attribution of criminal intent from ambiguous aerial movement.
evidence: Both Elizabeth and Cincinnati show 'suspicious person' as a top-3 mission type.

prediction: Autonomous flight transitions (approach, hover, orbit, return) create natural event boundaries that aid segmentation.
evidence: Chula Vista: Autonomous Skydio flight modes create explicit transition cues.

prediction: 34.3% nighttime flights degrade visual event segmentation quality.
evidence: Cincinnati: Over one-third of flights occur in low-light conditions.

prediction: 8,254 flights in 1 year represents high exposure enabling rapid automaticity development.
evidence: Cincinnati: High flight volume confirms rapid skill acquisition pathway.

prediction: 8.0% of flights exceeding 20-min threshold confirms vigilance decrement conditions.
evidence: Cincinnati: Quantitative threshold exceedance directly measured.

prediction: Most developed governance framework (General Order, privacy safeguards, audits) creates procedural legitimacy in policy.
evidence: Elizabeth: General Order Vol 3 Ch 18 provides the most comprehensive DFR governance framework of the three cities.
